subject: Chemistry
q:
A sample of gas is compressed from an initial volume of $2V_0$ to $V_0$ using three different processes.

First: Using reversible isothermal

Second: Using reversible adiabatic

Third: Using irreversible adiabatic under a constant external pressure, then :

Which of the following statements is correct?
question:
A sample of gas is compressed from an initial volume of $2V_0$ to $V_0$ using three different processes.

First: Using reversible isothermal

Second: Using reversible adiabatic

Third: Using irreversible adiabatic under a constant external pressure, then :

Which of the following statements is correct?
options: Final temperature of gas will be highest at the end of third process.
Final temperature of gas will be highest at the end of second process.
Enthalpy change of sample will be highest in isothermal process. (magnitude wise)
Final pressure of gas will be highest at the end of second process.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statements is not correct for a reversible process?
question: Which of the following statements is not correct for a reversible process?
options: The system stays in thermodynamic equilibrium throughout the process.
The process occurs at an extremely slow rate.
At any stage, the process can be reversed by applying an infinitesimally small change in the opposite direction.
Reversible processes can be carried out in a finite time.

q: A reaction cannot be spontaneous if
question: A reaction cannot be spontaneous if
options: both $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ have negative values.
$\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ have positive values.
$\Delta H$ has a negative value and $\Delta S$ has a positive value.
$\Delta H$ has a positive value and $\Delta S$ has a negative value.

Find the enthalpy of formation (in kcal/mol) of HI(g) using the data given below.

H2(g) + Cl2(g) → 2HCl(g): ΔH = -44.20 kcal

HCl(g) + aq → HCl(aq): ΔH = -17.31 kcal

HI(g) + aq → HI(aq): ΔH = -19.21 kcal

KOH(aq) + HCl(aq) → KCl(aq): ΔH = -13.74 kcal

KOH(aq) + HI(aq) → KI(aq): ΔH = -13.67 kcal

Cl2(g) + 2KI(aq) → 2KCl(aq) + I2(s): ΔH = -52.42 kcal

q:
For KCl, the Born-Haber cycle is considered using the following data

$$ \Delta_f H^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{KCl}=-436.7 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{Sub}} H^{\circ} \text { of } K=89.2 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

$\Delta_{\text {Ionization }} H^{\circ}$ for $K=419.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$; $\Delta_{\text {Electron gain }} H^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g})=-348.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$;

$$ \Delta_{\text {Bond }} H^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{Cl}_2=243.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

The value of the lattice enthalpy of KCl in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ . (Nearest integer)
